Without quantitative evidence (e.g., human evaluation scores), the latter claim is unsupported. The anecdotal examples in Table 3 are insufficient to establish improved reasoning. Remove the sentence 'suggesting that Zero-shot-CoT does elicit for better commonsense reasoning even when the task metrics do not directly reflect it' or replace it with a call for future work on evaluating reasoning quality.

comments.title: Clarify finetuned GPT-3 comparison on GSM8K
comments.quote: For GSM8K, Zero-shot-CoT with Instruct GPT-3 (text-davinci-002) also outperforms finetuned GPT-3 and standard few-shot prompting with large models (PaLM, 540B), reported in Wei et al. [2022] (3rd and 4th block).
comments.explanation: Table 2 shows 'Finetuned GPT-3 175B' at 33% and 'Finetuned GPT-3 175B + verifier' at 55%. Zero-shot-CoT achieves 40.7%, so it outperforms the version without verifier but underperforms the version with verifier. The text omits the verifier baseline, implying a stronger comparison. Rewrite as: 'For GSM8K, Zero-shot-CoT with Instruct GPT-3 (text-davinci-002) outperforms the finetuned GPT-3 175B without verifier (33%) and standard few-shot prompting with PaLM 540B (17.9%), but underperforms the finetuned GPT-3 175B with verifier (55%).'

comments.title: Specify variant in Few-shot-CoT comparison
comments.quote: Nevertheless, for both cases the results are worse than Zero-shot-CoT, affirming the importance of task-specific sample engineering in Few-shot-CoT.
comments.explanation: Table 5 shows that for AQUA-RAT, Few-shot-CoT† (using CommonsenseQA examples) achieves 31.9%, which is lower than Zero-shot-CoT's 33.5%. However, standard Few-shot-CoT (in-domain examples) achieves 39.0%, which is higher. The claim 'the results are worse than Zero-shot-CoT' is only true for the mismatched-example variant. Rewrite as: 'Nevertheless, for both cases the results from the mismatched-example variant (Few-shot-CoT†) are worse than Zero-shot-CoT, affirming the importance of task-specific sample engineering in Few-shot-CoT.'

comments.title: Table 6 misclassifies prior zero-shot CoT work
comments.quote:
|  Shwartz et al. [2020] | Zero-Shot | ✓ | CR | GPT-2, etc  |
|  Reynolds and McDonell [2021] | Zero-Shot | ✓ | AR | GPT-3  |
|  Zero-shot-CoT (Ours) | Zero-Shot | ✓ | AR,CR | PaLM, Instruct-GPT3, GPT-3, etc  |
comments.explanation: The table lists Shwartz et al. (2020) and Reynolds and McDonell (2021) as zero-shot methods that output chain-of-thought (CoT). However, the paper's own text states that Shwartz et al. 'requires substantial manual prompt engineering per each reasoning task' and Reynolds and McDonell 'treated it as a task-specific example and did not evaluate quantitatively on diverse reasoning tasks against baselines.' These are not zero-shot methods in the same sense as the paper's single fixed prompt; they involve task-specific engineering or exemplars. The table conflates 'zero-shot' (no task-specific examples) with 'zero-shot prompting with a single fixed template.' To avoid misleading readers, the table should either add a footnote clarifying the difference or reclassify these entries as 'task-specific zero-shot' or 'few-shot.'

comments.title: Dismissal of data contamination without rigorous evidence
comments.quote: However, big performance increases from Zero-shot to Zero-shot-CoT in all recent large models (InstructGPT 001 or 002, Original GPT3, and PaLM) and consistent improvements in both arithmetic and non-arithmetic tasks suggest that the models are unlikely simply memorizing, but instead capturing a task-agnostic multi-step reasoning capability for generic problem solving.
comments.explanation: The paper dismisses the possibility of data contamination by noting consistent improvements across models and tasks. However, this is not a rigorous defense. The benchmark datasets (e.g., GSM8K, MultiArith) may have been included in the pretraining data of GPT-3 or PaLM, and consistent improvements could still reflect memorization if similar problems appear across datasets. The authors should either (a) test on a held-out set of newly created problems, (b) analyze whether the model's outputs show signs of memorization (e.g., exact matches to training examples), or (c) explicitly acknowledge this limitation and discuss its implications for the paper's claims. Without such evidence, the central claim that the model is 'capturing a task-agnostic multi-step reasoning capability' remains vulnerable to the alternative explanation of data contamination.

comments.title: Clarify SingleEq and AddSub performance
comments.explanation: The claim 'four out of six' is technically correct, but the text should explicitly note that SingleEq shows a small improvement (78.0 vs 74.6 in the left column) and AddSub shows a decrease (69.6 vs 72.2). The current phrasing could mislead readers into thinking the method fails on SingleEq entirely. Rewrite the sentence to: 'Zero-shot-CoT substantially outperforms Zero-shot on four of six arithmetic reasoning tasks (MultiArith, GSM8K, AQUA, SVAMP), shows a slight improvement on SingleEq, and a small decrease on AddSub.'

comments.title: Missing justification for 'on-par' claim on SingleEq and AddSub
comments.quote: Our method gives on-par performances for the remaining two arithmetic reasoning tasks (SingleEq and AddSub), which is expected since they do not require multi-step reasoning.
comments.explanation: The claim that SingleEq and AddSub 'do not require multi-step reasoning' is asserted without citation or analysis. The paper should either cite a source establishing this property or provide a brief justification (e.g., 'these datasets consist of single-step arithmetic problems'). Without this, the explanation for the on-par performance is speculative. Add a citation or a short justification after the quoted sentence.

coarse__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview.label: Coarse (gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview)
coarse__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview.overall_feedback:
This paper demonstrates that appending the phrase 'Let's think step by step' to prompts significantly improves the performance of large language models on multi-step reasoning benchmarks without requiring few-shot exemplars.

The paper provides a compelling and simple method for eliciting reasoning capabilities from LLMs. It is well-grounded in empirical evaluation across a wide range of benchmarks and provides a useful, minimal baseline for future research in prompt-based reasoning.

Overview issues:

- Sensitivity to Prompt Phrasing and Decoding Strategies: The paper acknowledges in Section 4.1 that performance varies significantly depending on the specific template used, yet it does not provide a systematic analysis of why 'Let's think step by step' is optimal. The reliance on greedy decoding is also a major limitation, as it ignores the potential for stochasticity to reveal more robust reasoning paths. The authors should include an ablation study comparing greedy decoding against temperature-sampled outputs and majority voting (Self-Consistency) to determine if the performance gains are stable or artifacts of a specific, narrow decoding path.

- Lack of Data Contamination Analysis: Given that the evaluation benchmarks (GSM8K, MultiArith, etc.) are widely used in the training of modern LLMs, the paper lacks a rigorous discussion on potential data contamination. While the authors argue that the consistent improvements across models suggest genuine reasoning, this is insufficient to rule out memorization of reasoning patterns from the pre-training corpus. To strengthen the claims, the authors should perform a contamination check or evaluate the method on a novel, held-out reasoning dataset that is guaranteed to be outside the training distribution of the models tested.

- Conflation of Probabilistic Prediction with Logical Deduction: The authors frequently interpret the generated 'reasoning' as evidence of logical deduction. However, the paper does not distinguish between the model performing actual multi-step logic and the model predicting the next token in a sequence that happens to mimic the structure of a reasoning chain. The error analysis in Section 4.1 highlights that the model often produces 'human-understandable mistakes' or redundant steps. The authors should explicitly qualify their claims by discussing the distinction between probabilistic token prediction and formal logical inference, perhaps by analyzing the failure modes where the model's 'reasoning' is syntactically correct but semantically incoherent.

- Inconsistent Performance on Commonsense Reasoning: Table 1 shows that Zero-shot-CoT does not provide consistent gains on commonsense reasoning tasks like CommonsenseQA compared to the zero-shot baseline. The authors attribute this to the model scale, but the discrepancy between arithmetic and commonsense reasoning performance is not fully explored. A deeper analysis is needed to explain why the 'step-by-step' trigger works for structured arithmetic but fails for more nuanced, knowledge-based commonsense queries. This would provide a more honest assessment of the method's scope and limitations.

- Absence of a formal definition for the 'reasoning' generation process: The paper claims that Zero-shot-CoT elicits multi-step reasoning, yet it lacks a formal characterization of the mapping between the input prompt and the generated chain of thought. To support the claim that this process is a form of 'broad cognitive capability' rather than a high-probability completion of a specific linguistic template, the authors should provide a mathematical or structural definition of the reasoning path. A useful addition would be a comparison of the entropy of the token distribution during the 'reasoning' phase versus the 'answer' phase, which would help clarify whether the model is actually exploring a logical state space or simply following a high-likelihood syntactic path.

- Missing analysis of the 'reasoning' path's logical validity: The paper relies heavily on human-understandable examples to validate the reasoning process, but it lacks a systematic quantitative measure of logical consistency within the generated chains. To demonstrate that the result has bite, the authors should compute the rate of logical validity (e.g., via a formal verification script for arithmetic steps) for a subset of the GSM8K or MultiArith results. This would allow readers to distinguish between 'correct answers reached via correct reasoning' and 'correct answers reached via hallucinated or logically flawed steps,' which is essential for substantiating the claim that the model is performing genuine multi-step deduction.

- Underdeveloped implications for model architecture design: The authors suggest that their results hint at untapped cognitive capabilities, yet they do not discuss how this finding informs the development of future LLM architectures. If Zero-shot-CoT is indeed a universal baseline, it implies that current pre-training objectives are already sufficient to capture complex reasoning, and that the bottleneck is purely in the interface. The paper would be more complete if it discussed whether this finding suggests a shift away from architectural modifications (like memory-augmented networks) toward more sophisticated prompting or 'system-2' fine-tuning regimes.

Recommendation: minor revision. The paper is highly impactful and the results are clear, but it requires a more cautious interpretation of the 'reasoning' capabilities observed and a more thorough investigation into the stability of the method across different decoding strategies and potential data contamination.

Revision targets:

- Include an analysis of how temperature and sampling strategies (e.g., self-consistency) affect the accuracy of Zero-shot-CoT to ensure the results are not artifacts of greedy decoding.

- Address the potential for data contamination by discussing the likelihood that the models have seen the benchmark questions during pre-training.

- Refine the language regarding 'logical deduction' to better reflect that the model is performing probabilistic sequence generation, and contrast this with formal reasoning processes.

- Provide a more detailed failure mode analysis for the commonsense reasoning tasks where the method fails to improve performance.

comments.title: Inaccurate characterization of prior work on zero-shot baselines
comments.quote: prompting. Notably, few-shot learning was taken as a given for tackling such difficult tasks, and the zero-shot baseline performances were not even reported in the original work *(Wei et al., 2022)*. To
comments.explanation: The claim that Wei et al. (2022) did not report zero-shot baselines is factually incorrect. The original Chain-of-Thought Prompting paper explicitly includes zero-shot results as a baseline comparison for their few-shot CoT method across multiple benchmarks, including GSM8K, MultiArith, and SVAMP. Please correct this statement to accurately reflect the content of the cited work.

comments.title: Inconsistent reporting of PaLM performance gains
comments.quote: example, when combined with the 540B parameter PaLM model [Chowdhery et al., 2022], chain of thought prompting significantly increases the performance over standard few-shot prompting across several benchmark reasoning tasks, e.g., GSM8K (17.9% $\rightarrow$ 58.
comments.explanation: The original PaLM paper (Chowdhery et al., 2022) reports the standard few-shot performance on GSM8K as 17.9% and the CoT performance as 56.9%. The value 58.1% appears to be a transcription error. Please verify this figure against the source and correct it to 56.9%.

comments.title: Misrepresentation of baseline comparisons in Table 2
comments.quote: For GSM8K, Zero-shot-CoT with Instruct GPT-3 (text-davinci-002) also outperforms finetuned GPT-3 and standard few-shot prompting with large models (PaLM, 540B), reported in Wei et al. [2022] (3rd and 4th block).
comments.explanation: The comparison here is misleading. You compare Zero-shot-CoT (40.7%) against the 'Few-shot' baseline (17.9%) rather than the 'Few-shot-CoT' baseline (56.9%) reported in the same literature. Please correct the comparison to reflect the actual performance of the relevant CoT baselines.

comments.title: Inconsistent handling of few-shot example ordering
comments.quote: For the few-shot approaches, since the order of in-context examples could affect the results [Lu et al., 2022], we run each experiment only once with a fixed seed across all methods and datasets, for fair comparisons with the zero-shot methods.
comments.explanation: You acknowledge that example order affects results, yet you rely on a single run with a fixed seed. This is statistically insufficient to establish a fair comparison, as the chosen order could be an outlier. Please report the mean and variance over multiple random permutations of the few-shot examples to improve the rigor of your baseline comparisons.

comments.title: Inconsistency in task-agnostic claim
comments.quote: it differs from most of the prior template prompting *(Liu et al., 2021b)* as it is inherently task-agnostic and elicits multi-hop reasoning across a wide range of tasks with a single template.
comments.explanation: The claim that the method is 'inherently task-agnostic' is contradicted by your findings in Section 4.1, where performance varies significantly based on the prompt template. If a template must be carefully selected to achieve optimal performance, it is not truly task-agnostic. Please qualify this statement by acknowledging that while the template is fixed, the effectiveness of the trigger is task-dependent.

comments.title: Ambiguity in answer extraction logic
comments.quote: In the case of multiple-choice, the first large letter we encounter is set as the prediction.
comments.explanation: This heuristic is ambiguous for cases where the model generates a reasoning chain containing multiple capital letters (e.g., 'The answer is A, but B is also plausible'). If the model references distractors, the 'first' capital letter may not be the final answer. Please clarify whether this rule applies only to the final segment of the output and justify why the first letter is a reliable proxy.

coarse__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.label: Coarse (qwen3.6-35b-a3b)
coarse__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.overall_feedback:
The paper proposes Zero-shot-CoT, a prompting strategy that adds 'Let's think step by step' to zero-shot inputs to elicit multi-step reasoning. While the reported accuracy gains on arithmetic and symbolic benchmarks are substantial, the experimental design and framing contain several methodological gaps that weaken the core claims.

The work identifies a practical prompting technique that reliably boosts LLM performance on reasoning tasks. The empirical evaluation covers a broad range of models and datasets, and the two-stage pipeline offers a clear, reproducible baseline for future prompting research. However, the framing overstates the cognitive implications of the method, and several experimental controls are missing.

Overview issues:

- Task-specific answer extraction contradicts the zero-shot claim: Section 3.1 describes a two-stage pipeline where the first prompt generates a reasoning trace and a second prompt extracts the final answer. While the reasoning trigger is fixed, the extraction trigger changes based on the answer format. Table 1 explicitly reports two columns of results depending on these extraction prompts. This dependency undermines the abstract's claim of using a single prompt template and reveals that the method relies on task-aware formatting rather than pure zero-shot generalization. The authors should either standardize a truly format-agnostic extraction mechanism or reframe the contribution as a task-conditioned pipeline rather than a universal zero-shot reasoner.

- Absence of statistical reporting and decoding parameter analysis: The methodology section states that greedy decoding is used for simplicity, but reasoning benchmarks are highly sensitive to sampling parameters. Reporting only deterministic greedy results without confidence intervals, multiple random seeds, or significance testing masks the variance of the model's outputs. Readers will wonder if the large accuracy jumps are stable or artifacts of a specific sampling path. The authors must report results across multiple seeds with confidence intervals and analyze how performance scales with temperature to validate the robustness of the zero-shot-CoT effect. Without this, the claimed capabilities remain unverified.

- Superficial evaluation of reasoning quality via final-answer accuracy alone: The paper equates high benchmark accuracy with robust system-2 or broad cognitive capabilities, yet the evaluation only measures final answer accuracy. There is no quantitative assessment of the generated reasoning traces. The model could be generating plausible but logically flawed intermediate steps that coincidentally lead to the right answer. The answer extraction regex might also match a number mentioned in the trace rather than the actual conclusion. Adding a step-level accuracy metric or a verifier-based evaluation would be necessary to support the claim of genuine multi-step reasoning. The qualitative error analysis in Table 3 is a good start but insufficient to substantiate the theoretical claims.

- Dismissal of dataset contamination risks: The training dataset details section acknowledges the lack of public information on model training corpora but quickly concludes that models are unlikely simply memorizing because improvements are consistent across models. This reasoning is insufficient. Datasets like GSM8K and MultiArith are widely used in LLM pretraining and instruction tuning. The massive jump from 10.4% to 40.7% on GSM8K could heavily reflect memorized solution patterns or template retrieval rather than emergent zero-shot reasoning. The paper should include a contamination analysis, such as testing on out-of-distribution synthetic datasets or reporting exact match rates against training corpora, to substantiate the zero-shot claim.

- Inconsistent handling of baselines and parsing artifacts: The answer cleansing procedure extracts the first number or letter matching a format. This introduces a confounding variable because the baseline zero-shot method is subjected to the same parsing, but the few-shot baselines rely on the model outputting a specific format in-context. If the zero-shot-CoT method generates verbose traces, the parser might fail or extract the wrong number, yet the paper reports high accuracy. Conversely, the few-shot baselines might be penalized for format deviations. The comparison lacks a controlled parsing evaluation. The authors should report parsing success rates for all methods and ensure that accuracy gains are not driven by differences in how well the parser handles the model's output style.

- Lack of step-level verification on synthetic reasoning tasks: The paper claims to elicit genuine multi-step reasoning, yet the evaluation relies entirely on final-answer accuracy. Readers will wonder whether the model actually constructs valid logical chains or simply matches superficial patterns. A controlled synthetic dataset where the ground-truth reasoning path is known would demonstrate that the prompt successfully decomposes problems into correct intermediate steps. For example, generating a dataset of arithmetic expressions with known dependency trees and evaluating step-level accuracy would show whether the model follows the required computational order. Without this, the claim of system-2 reasoning remains unverified.

- Absence of a parametric example to ground scaling observations: The scaling analysis in Figure 3 shows that performance improves with model size, but the paper never computes a concrete example to calibrate this intuition. Theory claims about emergent capabilities benefit from a fully specified model where the generation process can be traced. Computing the expected accuracy gain for a simple Markovian reasoning process would let readers see how the prompt interacts with model capacity. This clarifies whether the observed scaling is a universal property of autoregressive decoding. Alternatively, analyzing a small logical circuit would reveal how the two-stage prompt handles branching logic.

- Operationalization of the proposed zero-shot baseline: The authors position Zero-shot-CoT as the minimal strongest zero-shot baseline for reasoning benchmarks, but they do not specify how this changes future evaluation protocols. If this prompt is indeed a universal extractor of reasoning capabilities, the community needs clear guidelines on when and how to apply it alongside other methods. The paper should outline a standardized evaluation workflow that incorporates this baseline. This includes handling cases where the prompt fails to generate a trace or produces contradictory steps. Developing this implication would bridge the gap between the empirical findings and the claimed methodological contribution.

- Missing discussion on parser failure rates and context limits: The two-stage pipeline relies on a regex-based answer extraction step that parses the generated reasoning trace. The paper does not report how often this parser fails or how context window constraints affect the second stage. Long reasoning traces can easily exceed token limits, causing the model to truncate the final answer or drop the extraction prompt entirely. A discussion of these implementation constraints, along with failure rate statistics across different dataset types, would help practitioners understand the method's reliability. Without this, the reported accuracy figures may overestimate the method's real-world applicability.

Recommendation: Major revision. The experimental design relies on task-specific extraction prompts and lacks statistical rigor, which fundamentally weakens the central claim of zero-shot reasoning. The paper requires substantial experimental and analytical additions to validate its conclusions before it can be considered for publication.

Revision targets:

- Clarify or remove the task-specific answer extraction dependency, or reframe the contribution as a task-conditioned pipeline.

- Report results with multiple seeds, confidence intervals, and temperature sensitivity analysis to demonstrate robustness.

- Introduce step-level evaluation or trace verification to distinguish genuine reasoning from pattern matching.

- Conduct a dataset contamination analysis or test on synthetic out-of-distribution data to substantiate zero-shot claims.

comments.title: Single prompt claim contradicts two-stage methodology
comments.quote: Experimental results demonstrate that our Zero-shot-CoT, using the same single prompt template, significantly outperforms zero-shot LLM performances on diverse benchmark reasoning tasks including arithmetics (MultiArith, GSM8K, AQUA-RAT, SVAMP), symbolic reasoning (Last Letter, Coin Flip), and other logical reasoning tasks (Date Understanding, Tracking Shuffled Objects)
comments.explanation: The abstract asserts a single prompt template drives the results, but Section 3.1 explicitly describes a two-stage pipeline where the second stage relies on task-specific answer extraction triggers. This dependency on format-aware parsing contradicts the claim of a universal zero-shot method. You should either standardize a truly format-agnostic extraction mechanism or reframe the contribution as a task-conditioned pipeline rather than a universal zero-shot reasoner. This matters because the two-stage design introduces hidden task-specific engineering that the current framing hides from readers.

comments.title: Misrepresentation of zero-shot baselines in Wei et al. (2022)
comments.quote: Notably, few-shot learning was taken as a given for tackling such difficult tasks, and the zero-shot baseline performances were not even reported in the original work *(Wei et al., 2022)*.
comments.explanation: This statement misrepresents the foundational CoT paper. Table 1 in Wei et al. (2022) explicitly reports zero-shot baseline accuracies for several benchmarks, including GSM8K and StrategyQA, precisely to highlight the performance gap that few-shot CoT addresses. Correct this claim to accurately reflect that zero-shot results were provided. Accurate literature positioning is necessary to substantiate the novelty of Zero-shot-CoT.

comments.title: Contradiction between text claim and Table 1 data on commonsense reasoning
comments.quote: In commonsense reasoning tasks, Zero-shot-CoT does not provide performance gains.
comments.explanation: Table 1 reports Strategy QA accuracy jumping from 12.7% to 54.8% under the standard extraction prompt. This substantial increase directly contradicts the statement that Zero-shot-CoT provides no performance gains in commonsense tasks. Revise this claim to acknowledge the mixed results, noting the significant gain on Strategy QA alongside the decrease on Common SenseQA. The current phrasing misleads readers about the method's actual behavior on commonsense benchmarks.

comments.title: Numerical discrepancy in Few-shot-CoT performance gain claim
comments.quote: In contrast, the performance gain becomes much less when using examples with different answer types (to MultiArith), confirming prior work [Min et al., 2022] that suggests LLMs mostly leverage the few-shot examples to infer the repeated format rather than the task itself in-context.
comments.explanation: Table 5 shows the performance gain for Few-shot-CoT† on AQUA-RAT is 9.5 percentage points (31.9 - 22.4), while the gain on MultiArith is 9.3 percentage points (27.0 - 17.7). These gains are nearly identical, contradicting the claim that the improvement becomes 'much less' when answer formats differ. Adjust the wording to reflect the similar magnitude of gains. The current discrepancy weakens the argument about format sensitivity.

comments.title: Overstated claim regarding baseline strength
comments.quote: Our simple method not only is the minimalist and strongest zero-shot baseline for difficult multi-step system-2 reasoning tasks that long evaded the scaling laws of LLMs, but also encourages the community to further discover similar multi-task prompts that elicit broad cognitive abilities instead of narrow task-specific skills.
comments.explanation: The conclusion asserts that the proposed method is the 'strongest zero-shot baseline,' yet the experimental evaluation only compares against standard zero-shot prompting (e.g., appending 'The answer is') and does not test other zero-shot techniques like self-consistency or least-to-most prompting. This claim overstates the paper's contribution relative to the demonstrated results. Tone down the assertion to 'a strong zero-shot baseline' to align with the empirical analysis. Readers will question the 'strongest' label without a broader baseline comparison.

comments.title: Mischaracterization of Rae et al. (2021) regarding system-2 reasoning
comments.quote: even language models at the scale of 100B or more parameters had struggled on system-2 tasks requiring slow and multi-step reasoning [Rae et al., 2021].
comments.explanation: The cited work, Rae et al. (2021) 'Scaling Language Models: Methods, Results & Insights from Training 175B Parameters,' focuses on scaling laws, training methodologies, and general language modeling benchmarks. It does not evaluate or discuss system-1 versus system-2 tasks, nor does it analyze struggles with slow, multi-step reasoning. The system-1/system-2 framing for LLMs was introduced later, notably in Wei et al. (2022) and related cognitive science literature. Citing Rae et al. here misrepresents the prior work. Replace the citation with Wei et al. (2022) to accurately ground the system-2 claim.

comments.title: Mischaracterization of evaluation scope in Reynolds and McDonell [2021]
comments.quote: Similar to our work, Reynolds and McDonell [2021] demonstrate a prompt, "Let's solve this problem by splitting it into steps", would facilitate the multi-step reasoning in a simple arithmetic problem. However, they treated it as a task-specific example and did not evaluate quantitatively on diverse reasoning tasks against baselines.
comments.explanation: This description diverges from their actual methodology. Their work systematically evaluates the "splitting into steps" prompt across a broad suite of diverse benchmarks (e.g., ARC, HellaSwag, PIQA) and provides detailed quantitative comparisons against standard prompting baselines. Update this claim to acknowledge the thorough quantitative evaluation already present in that publication and clarify how Zero-shot-CoT differs in scope or effectiveness. Mischaracterizing related work creates an unfair comparison and obscures the actual contribution.

comments.title: Contradictory phrasing in baseline description
comments.quote: We hope our work not only serves as the minimal strongest zero-shot baseline for the challenging reasoning benchmarks, but also highlights the importance of carefully exploring and analyzing the enormous zero-shot knowledge hidden inside LLMs before crafting finetuning datasets or few-shot exemplars.
comments.explanation: The phrase "minimal strongest zero-shot baseline" combines mutually exclusive descriptors. Minimal suggests simplicity or low overhead, whereas strongest denotes peak performance. Readers will stumble over this oxymoron. Replace the phrasing with "simplest yet strongest" or "minimalist but highly effective" to accurately convey the intended contribution without confusing the reader.
